prompt: Write a 150-word short story about a lighthouse keeper who discovers something unexpected in the fog. Do not use dialogue.
response:
The lighthouse keeper adjusted his spectacles as the fog rolled in, swallowing the ocean and the sky in its dense embrace. He lit the lamp, its steady rhythm cutting through the mural of gray. Another night alone, another vigil of isolation.

Hours passed as he watched the mists swirl. Then, through the fog, he noticed something. A soft, pulsing blue light. Not the warning flash of a ship, but something rhythmic, almost purposeful. He strained his eyes, descending the spiral staircase to stand on the rocky shore below. The fog thinned for a moment, revealing a circular clearing in the water, illuminated by the blue glow. From the center emerged shapes—smooth, organic forms gliding just beneath the surface, their movements synchronized with the light. They seemed to be... communicating. An ancient language of beams and currents, older than lighthouses, older than man. He stood transfixed, his lonely world expanding in an instant. When the fog returned, heavy and complete, the vision vanished. But the keeper knew. He was no longer just a guardian of the light. He was a witness.
